title: Secularism and coercive freedoms
abstract: The animus behind Judith Butler's investigation of the links between sexual politics, torture and secularism in the early twenty-first century is the perceived need for a ‘politics that opposes state coercion and violence’ (Butler 2008: 20). This is what drives her attempt to demolish the claim that the USA's military operations in the Middle East are delivering ‘civilization’ to the allegedly benighted heartlands of Islam. The nexus that she discerns between violence and sexuality in the torture of detainees at Abu Ghraib and Guantanamo has its roots, she argues, in questionable ideas about the secularism that is supposed to ground ‘modern’ politics. Her contention is that the secularism that is widely used to legitimate American claims to embody enlightened modernity is actually no more enlightened than the ‘worst forms of dogmatic and restrictive religion’ (Butler 2008: 19). In short, Butler's article is, by turns, provocative, radical and challenging to much of the received wisdom about religion and modernity, among other things. The article raises more questions than I can tackle here, so my reflections will deal only with selected aspects of the arguments about secularism and religion. I shall suggest that Judith Butler's analysis of secularism in the politics of the USA and France goes a long way towards identifying some key paradoxes and dilemmas but that her case could be strengthened if she took better account of the differences between forms of secularism that are enshrined in the laws of these countries. I shall concentrate my remarks on France. One of the characteristics of Judith Butler's position is a – sometimes disarming – degree of honesty. She is unflinchingly direct and clear about the dilemmas facing attempts to advocate a progressive sexual politics. She embraces the tension between a commitment to progressive sexual politics and a commitment to resist discrimination against religious minorities that propagate distinctly non-progressive views of sexuality. Moreover, she lays bare the limitations of pluralist and rights-based discourses that seem blind to the coercive properties of the states that advocate them. This makes for uncomfortable reading for liberal and progressive thinkers, for her contention is that ideas of freedom can all too easily serve as rationalizations of coercion. But, as I shall argue below, the symbolic order of secularism in France currently regards some forms of religion as an assault on freedom and reason. Within the compass of a single article, Butler could not have examined all the implications of her concern that the pursuit of certain freedoms may actually require the parallel defence of minorities with restrictive ideas about the meaning of freedom. This is an echo of the recurrent dilemma about the extent to which liberals should be tolerant of people who do not necessarily place a high value of tolerance and dialogue for their own sake. One wonders, for example, how Women Against Fundamentalism would react to Butler's apparently unqualified rejection of discrimination against religious minorities that might not support her resistance to homophobia. She wants to preserve a seamless web of struggles for progressive sexual politics and against‘racism and the discrimination against religious minorities’ (Butler 2008: 3). This raises the general question of whether all forms of discrimination are, by definition, anti-progressive. A more specific question would arise in connection with religious minorities that held racist views. These questions and dilemmas about the limits of toleration are far from being the exclusive preserve of political theorists and philosophers, as any prison administrator would attest. In total institutions like prisons someone has to decide whether lines need to be drawn – and, if so, where – between beliefs and practices that are permitted and those that are forbidden. The recent decision by officials of the Federal Bureau of Prisons in the USA to remove from prison chaplaincy libraries all books, tapes and videos that do not appear on a list of approved resources is a case in point.1 Tolerant chaplains do not welcome censorship, but higher level officials insist that the price of tolerance is too high when perceived threats to good order and security are real. Butler's ‘warts and all’ exposé of progressive politics and its discontents that flow from what she calls the ‘conceit of a progressive history’ is not only disarming: it can also be disorienting. Part of the reason for this is that she deliberately undermines any spatial or temporal certainties that locate modern notions of freedom or progress in the here and now of the West. Instead, she questions how far freedom has really expanded and how widely it has spread around the world. The comforting idea that progress is a cumulative process in which each newly acquired freedom becomes the platform from which to launch even more radical claims for justice or equality receives a severe jolt. Another reason for the disorienting effect of Butler's article is that it cuts across topics, discourses and literatures that rarely speak to each other: Dutch immigration controls, heterosexual normativity in French policies on gay parenting, the Pope's strictures on Islam, the complexities of secularism, and the paradoxical link between the torture of detainees in American camps and the civilizing mission attributed to the US military. But the thread that runs through these ostensibly disparate topics is clear and cogent, namely, they all demonstrate the coercive powers of apparently liberal states to legitimate themselves by discourses of freedom, modernity and cultural integration that actually mask varying degrees of violence, homophobia and misogyny. The Dutch case turns on the practice of asking applicants for immigration whether they are offended by photos of two men kissing and whether they really wish to live in a country that respects the rights of gay people to express their sexuality openly in public. The immigration authorities regard this as an appropriate part of an assessment of ‘civic integration’ on the grounds that modern life in the Netherlands presupposes the right to sexual freedom. Butler imagines that applicants who come from relatively affluent countries or who have relatively high levels of skills and income are exempt because they are considered unlikely to hold homophobic views. In other words, acceptance of sexual freedom has been turned into a precondition of the freedom to reside legally in the Netherlands. Butler's objection to this instrumentalization of sexual freedom as one of the defining characteristics of an approved set of cultural and social norms could, it seems to me, be applied by extension to the attempts made by many other states – including the UK since 9/11 – to establish social cohesion on the basis of cultural uniformity or shared values. This suggests that the much-vaunted freedom of capital and labour to flow through global circuits may actually be eliciting and reinforcing increasingly circumscribed notions of approved freedoms and rights. Ostensible diversification could ironically be homogenizing what it means to be modern and free. The case of France is especially interesting not only because of the Republic's resistance to gay parenting and the display of ostentatious symbols of religious identity in public institutions but also because successive French governments and parliamentary majorities have been energetically trying, since the late 1970s, to thwart the growth of minority religious movements. A long series of parliamentary reports and debates has led to two major measures. The first consists of the About-Picard law, passed in 2001, that can be used against religious movements deemed to ‘violate human rights and fundamental civil liberties’ and/or to abuse the psychological weaknesses of their recruits. The second is the creation, by presidential decrees, of three public bodies, one after the other, for monitoring the so-called excesses or aberrations of religious cults (‘dérives sectaires’). Not only has the resourcing and political support for these bodies been copious, but they have also been able to exercise considerable influence throughout the institutions of the Republic by stimulating, co-ordinating and monitoring their programmes for countering the threat that cultic aberrations allegedly pose to the well-being of citizens and the security of the state.2 Why is the French Republic going to such lengths to combat religious movements? The answer falls into two parts, both of which illustrate and elaborate on Judith Butler's arguments about the nexus of modern states, coercive freedom and cultural uniformity. One part of the answer lies with the constitutional principle that the Republic is one, indivisible and laic. In turn, this entails the well-known separation of religions from the state. More importantly, the same principle has also engendered the practice of cultivating a strictly secular ethos in the state's systems of education, healthcare, social welfare, law enforcement and the armed forces. This amounts to much more than merely excluding expressions of religion from the public sphere: it comes close to being a secular form of civil religion. One effect of French laïcité is to frame all other forms of religion as a matter only for the private or associational sphere of life. Consequently, religious movements that mobilize their followers to try to influence public – especially political – life are accused of illicitly acting according to a logic that is ‘communal’ and, therefore, divisive. The unity between the Republic and its individual citizens is said to be threatened by such communalism. The second part of the answer to the question of why France is so preoccupied with the threats attributed to minority religious movements has to do with another facet of laïcité, namely, the assumption that only reason and rationality are sufficiently robust, objective and universal to function as the cultural and cognitive foundations of a secular republic (Beckford 2004). The capacity for rational thinking is hindered, according to this view, if citizens do not enjoy the freedom of thought that can be cultivated in strictly secular schools but not in institutions influenced by religion. In other words, the ‘default value’ of reason and rationality is assumed to be secular. Opposition to religious movements is strong in France precisely because they are suspected of somehow undermining the freedom of thought that is a prerequisite for rational thinking. And, without the capacity to make a free choice of religion, how can any follower of a religious movement claim to have made a rational decision to join? It seems to me that the French state's discourse about minority religions tries to instrumentalize notions of the free thinking and rationality in such a way that minority religious movements can be categorized as, by definition, opposed to freedom and reason. In other words, the ‘aberrations’ or ‘dérives’ of which religious movements stand accused allegedly represent a drift away from the cultural norm of secularity. Nor surprisingly, then, the current antagonism towards religious movements in France reaches a climax when politicians examine the education of children whose parents belong to such movements. The National Assembly report on an ‘Inquiry into the influence of cultic movements and the consequences of their practices for the physical and mental health of minors’3 makes it clear that the very idea of home schooling within a cultic environment is considered an offence against secularist rationalism. It clearly challenges what Judith Butler calls the ‘ordre symbolique’. The secularism of the USA is, by contrast, more muted and more narrowly focused on separating the state from religions. Indeed, there is much truth in the old adage that the separation of church and state in the USA has generally protected religious organizations from the state, whereas in France the purpose of the separation has always been to protect the state from religions. As a result, relations between most forms of religion and the American state are relatively relaxed and mutually supportive – if not too close for comfort (Hamilton 2005). In fact, the US Department of State has been actively promoting its notion of religious freedom around the world since the 1940s. This is in sharp contrast to the French state's understanding of secularism. Not surprisingly, then, the annual reports that the US State Department publishes have often been critical of France's treatment of religious minorities. Equally unsurprising is the fact that French ripostes to these reports have been critical of American naivety with regard to the apparent dangers posed by many religious movements. These diplomatic skirmishes underline the necessity to recognize the variety of forms that secularism takes in the modern world. If the French variant elides Reason with raison d'état, the American variant confirms Tocqueville's observations about the individualistic and voluntaristic character of reason and freedom that prevails in the USA. But each country undoubtedly regards its ‘take’ on reason, religion and freedom as part of its civilizing mission. Secular ideals are central to both missions, but their respective meanings of ‘secular’ are quite different. In sum, Judith Butler is right to detect the influence of secularism on the selection of the freedoms that are presumed to constitute the modernity of the technologically advanced countries. I have tried to show the differences between the American and the French implementations of secularism.
